items.question_text: After communications are established with a U.S. civil aircraft using its registration number, how may the identification be abbreviated?

choices.text: Use the identification prefix and the last three digits or letters.
Use only the last two digits or letters once the pilot has replied.
Drop the prefix and use any shortened form that sounds distinct.
Keep the full registration until the aircraft lands.

items.explanation: A U.S. civil registration may be abbreviated to the identification prefix and the last three digits or letters after communications are established.

items.question_text: Which identification may not be abbreviated after communications are established?

choices.text: A similar-sounding aircraft identification.
A U.S. civil registration that has already been established on frequency.
A civil registration with three trailing letters.
Any registration once the pilot has read it back once correctly.

items.explanation: Similar-sounding identifications are not abbreviated.

items.situation: N123AB has been on frequency long enough to allow abbreviation, but a second aircraft with a similar-sounding identification checks in. Another controller wants to keep using abbreviated replies to both aircraft to save time.
items.para_context: Some U.S. civil registrations may be abbreviated after communications are established, but similar-sounding identifications may not be abbreviated.

choices.text: Stop using abbreviated replies for the similar-sounding aircraft and use full identification to keep the call signs distinct.
Keep abbreviating both because once communications are established abbreviation always takes priority over clarity.
Abbreviate only the second aircraft because it checked in later.
Refer to the aircraft by type alone instead of using either call sign until one leaves the frequency.

items.explanation: Clarity overrides the convenience of abbreviation when identifications sound alike.

items.question_text: Which interphone transmission priority comes immediately after emergency messages?

choices.text: Clearances and control instructions.
Flight plan messages.
Routine progress reports.
Administrative coordination.

items.explanation: Clearances and control instructions are the next priority after emergency messages.

items.question_text: After an actual emergency has passed, may messages relating to that accident continue to receive emergency priority?

choices.text: No. They receive lower priority.
Yes. They keep emergency priority until the aircraft reaches the gate.
Yes. Any message tied to an emergency event permanently keeps first priority.
No. They may not be transmitted at all once the emergency has passed.

items.explanation: Once the actual emergency is over, related messages receive lower priority.

items.situation: An emergency message is waiting to be passed on the interphone. At the same time, another controller is trying to coordinate a routine progress-report update.
items.para_context: Emergency messages have the highest interphone priority. Clearances and control instructions follow. Lower-priority traffic does not go first simply because it was ready first.

choices.text: Handle the emergency message first and delay the lower-priority progress-report update.
Handle the progress report first because it was ready before the emergency message arrived.
Alternate between the two messages so neither controller has to wait.
Send the progress report first unless the emergency aircraft has already declared Mayday on the radio frequency.

items.explanation: Interphone transmission order is based on message priority, not who started speaking first.

items.question_text: Which words may be used to interrupt lower-priority interphone messages when necessary?

choices.text: "Emergency" or "control."
"Priority" or "attention."
"Urgent" or "break."
"Stop" or "override."

items.explanation: The prescribed interruption words are "emergency" or "control."

items.question_text: May a controller interrupt a lower-priority interphone message with the word "priority" instead of the prescribed interruption words?

choices.text: No. Use the prescribed words "emergency" or "control."
Yes. Any word that sounds urgent may replace the prescribed words.
Yes, but only if the other controller already knows the subject of the interruption.
No. The only approved interruption word is "emergency."

items.explanation: The order specifies the interruption words rather than leaving them to local preference.

items.situation: Another controller is in the middle of a low-priority interphone message. You need to break in immediately with a higher-priority control coordination that is not an emergency.
items.para_context: To interrupt lower-priority interphone messages when necessary, use the words "emergency" or "control."

choices.text: Interrupt with the word "control" and pass the higher-priority coordination.
Wait until the lower-priority message finishes, because only emergencies justify interruption.
Interrupt with the word "priority" because it sounds less abrupt than "control."
Switch to the radio frequency and call the controller there instead of interrupting the interphone message.

items.explanation: A higher-priority control message may interrupt a lower-priority one using the prescribed interruption word.

items.question_text: When may gate or fix names be substituted for interphone position identification?

choices.text: Only when the substitution is operationally beneficial and covered by an LOA or facility directive.
Any time the caller believes a gate or fix name will be shorter.
Only between positions in the same building, with no additional documentation needed.
Only when neither position uses a numeric or titled line name.

items.explanation: Alternative interphone identifiers are allowed only under controlled, documented conditions.

items.situation: You are calling Washington Approach from Washington Center on the Fifty Seven line. Build the caller identification.
items.target_phrase: WASHINGTON CENTER WASHINGTON APPROACH ON THE FIFTY SEVEN LINE

items.situation: Two positions have begun interphone coordination, but the caller starts with a shorthand local nickname that could be confused with another position on the same network.
items.para_context: Caller and receiver identify their facility and/or position clearly enough to avoid confusion with another position. Gate or fix names are substituted only when operationally beneficial and covered by an LOA or facility directive.

choices.text: Restart with a clear facility and/or position identification that avoids confusion.
Continue with the shorthand nickname because speed matters more than distinct identification.
Use any gate or fix name even without an LOA if both controllers think it is obvious.
Skip position identification entirely and move straight to the coordination message.

items.explanation: The format begins with clear caller/receiver identification, not an ambiguous local shortcut.

items.question_text: How are interphone messages terminated?

choices.text: With operating initials.
With the word "over."
With the receiving position number.
With the current UTC time.

items.explanation: Operating initials are the standard interphone-message termination.

items.question_text: How should a normal interphone message be terminated?

choices.text: With operating initials.
With the word "over."
With the receiving facility name repeated back.
With the message source stated again.

items.explanation: The expected interphone closing is operating initials, not a radio-style closing word.

items.situation: A controller finishes an interphone coordination with the word "over" and no operating initials because that feels more natural from radio work.
items.para_context: Interphone messages are terminated with operating initials.

choices.text: Terminate interphone messages with operating initials instead of a radio-style "over."
Use "over" for all interphone messages, because it is clearer than initials.
Use neither initials nor "over" if the message was short.
Repeat the receiving position title instead of using operating initials.

items.explanation: Interphone coordination ends with operating initials, not with the radio word "over."

items.question_text: When may an en route controller omit the words super or heavy?

choices.text: They may be omitted en route except in the specifically listed cases where they still must be used.
They may always be omitted once radar contact is established.
They may never be omitted in any en route communication.
They may be omitted only when the pilot requests shorter phraseology.

items.explanation: The paragraph allows limited en route omission, not universal omission.

items.situation: You are addressing United 58 Heavy. Build the call sign using approved phraseology.
You are coordinating with a terminal facility about a heavy aircraft operation. Another controller wants to omit the word heavy because the discussion is taking place in the en route environment.
items.para_context: En route omission of super or heavy is limited and does not apply in every communication, including all listed exceptions such as communications with or about certain terminal operations and traffic advisories. Air Force One and Air Force Two are not given the heavy designator.

choices.text: Use the heavy designator if the communication falls within one of the listed exceptions instead of omitting it automatically.
Omit heavy because any en route controller may omit it in every communication.
Replace heavy with super because the aircraft is already known to be large.
Add heavy even to Air Force One if the aircraft weight supports it.

items.explanation: Omission is conditional, not universal, and presidential call signs are a separate exception.

items.question_text: What should not be done with similar sounding aircraft identifications?

choices.text: They should not be abbreviated.
They should not be acknowledged on frequency.
They should not be answered by the same controller.
They should not be paired with any special pilot notification.

items.explanation: Similar-sounding aircraft identifications are not abbreviated.

items.situation: Two similar call signs are on frequency and you need to emphasize the first one clearly. Build the emphasized call sign.

Two similar call signs are operating simultaneously in the same sector. Another controller wants to keep using abbreviated replies because both pilots have already checked in and the frequency is busy.
items.para_context: When similar-sounding call signs are operating simultaneously, notify each pilot concerned and do not abbreviate the identifications. Emphasis may be used to keep them distinct.

choices.text: Notify the pilots concerned and avoid abbreviation of the similar-sounding identifications.
Keep abbreviating because once communications are established the risk of confusion is considered resolved.
Refer to the aircraft by type or color instead of the call signs until one leaves the sector.
Delay all transmissions to one aircraft until the other aircraft changes frequency.

items.explanation: The paragraph addresses simultaneous duplicate or similar call signs by notification, clarity, and no abbreviation.

items.question_text: Which pronunciation standard should be used for numbers and individual letters?

choices.text: ICAO pronunciation.
Any local pronunciation if the pilot sounds domestic.
FAA-only pronunciation for numbers and ICAO only for letters.
Plain conversational English instead of a standard phonetic system.

items.explanation: Numbers and individual letters use the ICAO pronunciation standard.

items.question_text: Which pronunciation must be used for numbers and individual letters?

choices.text: ICAO phonetics.
Local pronunciation whenever the pilot and controller share the same first language.
Military phonetics for letters and ICAO only for numbers.
Any clear pronunciation as long as the word is spoken slowly.

items.explanation: The standard is fixed: use ICAO phonetics for numbers and individual letters.

items.situation: A controller is tempted to use plain-English letter names and casual number pronunciation because the pilot is domestic and the exchange seems simple.
items.para_context: Numbers and individual letters use ICAO phonetics.

choices.text: Use ICAO phonetics for the numbers and individual letters instead of switching to casual local pronunciation.
Switch to plain-English pronunciation because the pilot is domestic and already understands the message.
Use local pronunciation for letters only and ICAO for numbers.
Use conversational pronunciation whenever the call sign contains fewer than four characters.

items.explanation: The standard does not depend on how easy the exchange feels; it remains ICAO phonetics.

items.question_text: After communications are established with a U.S. civil aircraft using its registration number, how may the identification be abbreviated?
items.explanation: Use the identification prefix and the last three digits or letters.

choices.text: Use the identification prefix and the last three digits or letters.
Use any shortened form that remains understandable.
Use only the last two digits or letters.
Do not abbreviate until after landing.

items.question_text: Which identification is one example that must not be abbreviated?
items.explanation: Similar-sounding identifications may not be abbreviated.

choices.text: A similar-sounding aircraft identification.
A U.S. civil registration that has already been established on frequency.
A civil registration ending in only three letters.
A registration that includes a manufacturer name.

items.question_text: A foreign-registered civil aircraft identification may be abbreviated after communications are established.
items.explanation: Foreign civil aircraft identifications are not abbreviated.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Which interphone transmission priority comes immediately after emergency messages?
items.explanation: Clearances and control instructions are next after emergency messages.

choices.text: Clearances and control instructions.
Routine progress reports.
Administrative coordination.
Flight plan filing updates.

items.question_text: After an actual emergency has passed, what priority should messages relating to that accident receive?
items.explanation: Once the emergency has passed, those messages receive lower priority.

choices.text: Lower priority.
Top priority until the aircraft reaches the gate.
The same emergency priority until all reports are complete.
No priority at all because follow-up messages may not be transmitted.

items.question_text: Emergency messages are the highest-priority interphone transmissions.
items.explanation: Emergency messages are the top interphone transmission priority.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Which words may be used to interrupt lower-priority interphone messages when necessary?
items.explanation: The prescribed interruption words are "emergency" or "control."

choices.text: "Emergency" or "control."
"Priority" or "attention."
"Break" or "urgent."
"Immediate" or "override."

items.question_text: If the interruption is for a higher-priority control message rather than an emergency, which prescribed word applies?
items.explanation: Use the word "control" for a higher-priority control interruption that is not an emergency.

choices.text: "Control."
"Priority."
"Break."
"Emergency."

items.question_text: A controller may replace the prescribed interruption words with any urgent-sounding word that seems clear on frequency.
items.explanation: The order specifies the interruption words instead of leaving them open-ended.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: When may gate or fix names be substituted for interphone position identification?
items.explanation: Such substitutions are allowed only when operationally beneficial and covered by an LOA or facility directive.

choices.text: Only when operationally beneficial and covered by an LOA or facility directive.
Any time the caller thinks the alternate name is shorter.
Only when both positions are inside the same building.
Only if the receiver repeats the message back correctly.

items.question_text: Complete the prescribed receiver approval example: "RECEIVER AMERICAN TWENTY-FIVE FLIGHT LEVEL THREE FIVE ZERO _____ AC"
items.explanation: The approved transmission ends with "APPROVED AC."

choices.text: APPROVED

items.question_text: How should caller and receiver identify themselves at the start of interphone coordination?
items.explanation: They identify their facility and/or position clearly enough to avoid confusion with another position.

choices.text: Clearly enough to avoid confusion with another facility or position.
Only by first names or local nicknames once the line is familiar to both controllers.
Only by the receiving position, since the caller is implied by the line in use.
By skipping identification and moving directly to the message when the coordination is urgent.

items.question_text: How are interphone messages terminated?
items.explanation: Interphone messages are terminated with operating initials.

choices.text: With operating initials.
With the word "over."
With the receiving facility name.
With the UTC time.

items.question_text: Which ending should replace a radio-style "over" on a normal interphone message?
items.explanation: Interphone coordination ends with operating initials.

choices.text: Operating initials.
The receiving position number.
The line number being used.
No ending at all.

items.question_text: Interphone messages are terminated with operating initials.
items.explanation: That is the required interphone message termination.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: How must the word heavy be used in communications with or about heavy aircraft?
items.explanation: Use the word heavy as part of the identification in communications with or about heavy aircraft, subject to the listed omission exceptions.

choices.text: Use heavy as part of the identification, subject to the listed omission exceptions.
Use heavy only if the pilot requests it.
Never use heavy in controller-to-controller communications.
Use heavy only on initial contact, then omit it automatically.

items.question_text: When communicating with Air Force One or Air Force Two, should ATC add the heavy designator to the call sign?
items.explanation: Do not add the heavy designator; state only Air Force One or Air Force Two.

choices.text: No. State only Air Force One or Air Force Two.
Yes, if the aircraft qualifies as heavy by weight.
Yes, but only in controller-to-controller coordination.
No, unless the aircraft is on final approach.

items.question_text: En route controllers may omit super or heavy in all communications, including communications with terminal facilities and traffic advisories.
items.explanation: En route omission is limited and does not apply in all communications.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: What must be done when similar-sounding aircraft identifications are on frequency together?
items.explanation: Notify each pilot concerned and do not abbreviate the similar-sounding identifications.

choices.text: Notify each pilot concerned and do not abbreviate the similar-sounding identifications.
Abbreviate both identifications as soon as the aircraft have acknowledged once.
Assign temporary numeric call signs instead of using the filed identifications.
Delay responses to one aircraft until the other leaves the sector.

items.question_text: Complete the prescribed duplicate-call-sign instruction: "CALL SIGNS WHEN THE AIRCRAFT ARE OPERATING _____ WITHIN THE SAME SECTOR"
items.explanation: The approved wording uses "SIMULTANEOUSLY."

choices.text: SIMULTANEOUSLY

items.question_text: Which call-sign practice is prohibited when similar-sounding identifications are involved?
items.explanation: Similar-sounding identifications are not abbreviated.

choices.text: Abbreviating them.
Notifying each affected pilot.
Using emphasis for clarity.
Using the full identification in replies.

items.question_text: Which pronunciation standard is used for numbers and individual letters?
items.explanation: Numbers and individual letters use ICAO phonetics.

choices.text: ICAO phonetics.
Local pronunciation.
Military phonetics.
Plain conversational English.

items.question_text: A controller may switch to plain-English letter names once the pilot is domestic and communications are established.
items.explanation: The standard remains ICAO phonetics for numbers and individual letters.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: What standard should be used when transmitting individual letters in a call sign or code?
items.explanation: Use ICAO phonetics for individual letters.

choices.text: ICAO phonetics.
Any pronunciation familiar to the pilot.
Military phonetics only if the aircraft is civilian.
Spoken letter names without phonetics.
